name: PAS
structure: Problem → Agitate → Solution
power_hook: Opens with pain, earns attention before the pitch
when_to_use: Best for cold audiences who feel a specific pain but haven't found a solution. Use when your customer has a clearly defined problem you can name and amplify. Most effective at awareness levels 2–3.
why_it_works: Pain-first structure triggers an emotional response immediately. By agitating the problem before presenting the solution, you make the relief feel more valuable. The brain craves resolution — PAS delivers it at exactly the right moment.

name: BAB
structure: Before → After → Bridge
power_hook: Transformation contrast stops the scroll instantly
when_to_use: Use when you have a clear, dramatic transformation to show. Strong for products with a visible before/after — weight loss, skin improvement, business results, financial outcomes.
why_it_works: The human brain is wired to spot contrast. Showing the before and after side-by-side forces the audience to imagine themselves in the 'after' state — and then bridges them to how to get there.

name: Social Proof
structure: Proof number → testimonials → CTA
power_hook: Other people's trust transfers directly to the viewer
when_to_use: Use when your audience is warm and comparing options. Most effective once they know what you offer but haven't committed. Works best at awareness levels 3–4.
why_it_works: People look to others to know what's safe to do — especially for purchases. Showing real numbers and verified testimonials removes risk and builds credibility faster than any claim you make yourself.

name: Direct Offer
structure: Bold claim → guarantee → CTA
power_hook: Eliminates the final objection with a bold guarantee
when_to_use: Use for hot, high-intent audiences who are already convinced of the category. Works best at levels 4–5 — people who need a push to act, not an education on why.
why_it_works: Hot audiences don't need convincing — they need clarity and confidence. A direct, no-risk offer with a guarantee eliminates the last objection standing between intent and purchase.

name: Listicle
structure: X reasons/benefits → numbered list → CTA
power_hook: Numbered benefits communicate completeness at a glance
when_to_use: Use when you have multiple strong benefits that each stand alone. Great for audiences who research before buying and appreciate seeing full specificity before committing.
why_it_works: Lists are cognitively easy to process — the brain knows how many points remain. Numbered benefits create a sense of completeness and signal that this product covers all bases.

name: Question Hook
structure: Provocative question → answer → CTA
power_hook: A direct question demands a mental response before scrolling
when_to_use: Use when your audience questions conventional wisdom in your space. Works best at levels 2–3, when people haven't found the right solution yet and are open to a better approach.
why_it_works: A question that challenges a belief the audience already holds creates a pattern interrupt. The brain must process and answer the question before it can move on — you have their attention.

name: Comparison
structure: Our way vs old way → we win → CTA
power_hook: Side-by-side data makes the decision obvious
when_to_use: Use when your product has objectively measurable advantages. Effective at levels 3–4 when the audience is actively comparing alternatives before making a decision.
why_it_works: Structured comparisons do the decision-making work for the viewer. By framing the data in your favor, you remove ambiguity and make your product the obvious logical choice.

name: Result First
structure: Specific result → how → CTA
power_hook: Specific results create instant credibility — features don't
when_to_use: Use when you have a specific, credible result to lead with. Best for products with measurable outcomes — revenue, weight loss, skin clarity, time saved. Works at levels 3–4.
why_it_works: Leading with a concrete result triggers immediate credibility assessment. If the number is specific and believable, it bypasses skepticism faster than any feature or benefit headline.

name: Empathy
structure: We understand → here is why → solution → CTA
power_hook: Feeling understood is the prerequisite for being persuaded
when_to_use: Use for very cold audiences who feel misunderstood or have tried alternatives without success. Especially effective for health, wellness, and personal development categories.
why_it_works: People buy from those who understand them. When someone feels seen and heard, they lower their guard. Empathy earns the emotional permission to make a recommendation — without it, you're just another ad.

name: Bold Statement
structure: Product hero → benefit callouts → offer → CTA
power_hook: Product as the hero with benefits radiating outward
when_to_use: Use when your product has a clear hero feature or visible differentiator best shown visually. Works well for physical products across levels 2–4.
why_it_works: The product becomes the answer. By placing benefits around it like satellites, you communicate comprehensiveness without requiring the viewer to read a paragraph. Visual authority — no explanation needed.

name: Sticky Note
structure: Authentic sticky note on product → benefit → CTA
power_hook: Looks personal and unpolished — the opposite of an ad
when_to_use: Use when you want to appear native and organic in a feed. Effective for building trust with skeptical cold audiences at levels 1–3 who are sensitive to advertising.
why_it_works: The brain dismisses polished ads on sight. A sticky note reads as a personal recommendation — something real, not produced. Authenticity lowers the guard before the message lands.

name: iPhone Notes
structure: iPhone Notes app screenshot → insight/list → CTA
power_hook: Screenshots feel shared by a friend, not sold by a brand
when_to_use: Use for audiences that trust peer-to-peer communication over marketing. Particularly effective for educational or informational products at levels 1–3.
why_it_works: Screenshots of phone interfaces feel like forwarded content — something a friend shared. The informal format creates the illusion of an insider tip, not a pitch.

name: iMessage
structure: Leaked chat conversation → product discovery → CTA
power_hook: A conversation you're not in feels more believable than an ad
when_to_use: Use when your best marketing comes from how customers recommend you to each other. Strong for products with word-of-mouth potential or categories where social proof travels fast.
why_it_works: Overhearing a conversation feels more authentic than being advertised to. The reader becomes a voyeur of an organic recommendation — and organic recommendation is the most trusted form of social proof.

name: ChatGPT Ad
structure: AI conversation reveals insight → CTA
power_hook: Frames your product as the AI-recommended choice
when_to_use: Use with tech-savvy or business audiences who use AI tools regularly. Creates the perception that an objective third party has evaluated your product category.
why_it_works: AI is perceived as neutral and authoritative. When ChatGPT appears to recommend a solution, the audience treats it like expert analysis rather than advertising — skepticism drops.

name: Us vs Them
structure: Us vs Them split → we win every dimension → CTA
power_hook: Binary framing forces a choice — and your side always wins
when_to_use: Use when you are clearly superior on specific, quantifiable dimensions. Works for warm audiences at levels 3–4 who are actively comparing options before deciding.
why_it_works: Binary thinking simplifies decisions. By splitting the visual into us-vs-them with clear data, you eliminate the middle ground and force the audience into a clear preference — yours.

name: Benefit Callout
structure: X benefits headline → numbered list + product → CTA
power_hook: Cumulative benefits build an irresistible value case
when_to_use: Use for warm audiences in research mode who need to see all the reasons before committing. Best at levels 3–4 when the audience is evaluating comprehensively.
why_it_works: Numbered benefits with specifics create a sense of thoroughness. Each benefit adds cumulative weight — by the time they reach the last point, the value case feels complete and overwhelming.

name: UGC Static
structure: Authentic user in real setting → casual result → CTA
power_hook: Real people in real settings kill ad blindness instantly
when_to_use: Use when you want to stop the scroll with authenticity. Highly effective for beauty, health, and lifestyle categories where peer validation drives purchase decisions.
why_it_works: User-generated content bypasses ad blindness entirely. A real person in a real environment is the visual equivalent of a trusted friend — not a marketing department.

name: Cartoon Style
structure: Illustrated scene → message → CTA
power_hook: Illustration stops the scroll — every other ad is a photograph
when_to_use: Use for cold audiences who respond to playfulness rather than professionalism. Effective for brands targeting younger demographics or concepts that are difficult to photograph.
why_it_works: Illustration stands out in a feed of photography. The style signals creativity and personality — and when the concept has humor or warmth, that positive emotion transfers directly to the brand.

name: Lifestyle Context
structure: Aspirational lifestyle scene → minimal copy → CTA
power_hook: The lifestyle sells itself — minimal copy keeps the dream intact
when_to_use: Use for aspirational products where the context of use is as powerful as the product itself. Best at levels 2–3 when the desired outcome or identity is the primary purchase driver.
why_it_works: Aspirational imagery sells the identity, not the product. The viewer doesn't want the watch — they want to be the person wearing it. Minimal copy keeps the aspiration pure and uninterrupted.

name: Carousel Ad
structure: Hook panel → story panels → result → CTA
power_hook: Sequential narrative rewards attention — and builds to a click
when_to_use: Use when your story requires more than one frame. Effective for multi-step transformations, product ranges, or educational content that rewards the audience for spending time.
why_it_works: Sequential storytelling creates micro-commitment — each card reveals more, encouraging the viewer to keep swiping. By the final card, they have invested enough attention to click through.

name: Review
structure: Dramatic photo + floating review card → CTA
power_hook: Dramatic visual + specific testimonial = persuasion at full power
when_to_use: Use when you have strong testimonials with specificity — names, outcomes, real numbers. Works at levels 3–4 where social proof is the final conversion lever before purchase.
why_it_works: A dramatic visual catches the eye; a floating review card catches the mind. The contrast between an attention-grabbing scene and a credible, specific testimonial creates maximum persuasion density.

name: Negative/Positive
structure: Negative now (red/dark) vs positive after (green/bright) → CTA
power_hook: Red and green communicate the transformation before text is read
when_to_use: Use when both the pain of the current situation and the pleasure of the outcome are emotionally strong. Works best at levels 2–3 for audiences who have already tried alternatives.
why_it_works: Color psychology is immediate — red reads as danger and negativity, green reads as safety and growth. The split communicates the entire transformation before the viewer processes a single word.
